Farn Por-rrrcal PnacrICES CouutssloN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
February 19,2004
George C. Spanog Deputy Attorney General
State of California
Department of Justice
Post Office 8ox944255
Sacramento, CA 94244-2550
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-04-025
Dear Mr. Spanos:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Transportation
Commission and Kirk Lindsay,r amernber of the California Transportation Commission,
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").z
Please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct.
(Regulation I 83 29(b)(8XA).)
QUESTTONS
May Commissioner Lindsey participate in the following decisions
1. Whether the Transportation Commission should reconsider the proposed
conveyance of excess propert/
2. If yes, whether the Transportation Commission should approve conveyance to
Flying J, Inc.?
CONCLUSIONS
1 - 2. Commissioner Lindsey may participate in these decisions provided it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will materially affect Commissioner Lindsey's
economic interests. (See discussion.)
t You a.e authorized by Commissioner Lindsey to request advice on this matter.
' Gorerrme.rt Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commiision regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
ary$.41


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 2
FACTS
The following facts are based on your correspondence and phone conversations
with Commission staff counsel.
You have requested advice regarding a property conveyance by the California
Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"). Although the California Transportation
Commission and Caltrans are separate state agencies, state law requires that any
conveyance by Caltrans of excess property be approved by the Transportation
Commission.
In February 2003, Caltrans asked the Transportation Commission to approve an
excess property conveyance to Flying J, Inc. The proposed conveyance was an element
of a contract between Caltrans and Flying J, [nc. The Transportation Commission did not
approve the conveyance, and instead voted (by a vote of 6 to 1) to have the property put
out for competitive bidding.
In May 2003, Caltrans conducted an auction of the property,located in Mojave
near an interchange. The bidders were informed that the auction sale was the subject of
litigation involving Calkans and Flying J, [nc. and was also subject to approval by the
Transportation Commission. The high bidder was a mernber of the public, Thomas
Pistacchio.
Due to the pending litigation, Caltrans has not asked for approval of any
conveyance of the property to Mr. Pistacchio. In addition, a request has been made that
the Transportation Commission reconsider its February 2003 decision.
The agenda for the Transportation Commission's February 26,2004, meeting
includes consideration of two related items. The first item is whether the Transportation
Commission should reconsider the proposed conveyance to Mr. Pistacchio. The second
itern, which assumes that the first itern is answered in the affirmative, is whether the
Transportation Commission should approve the proposed conveyance to Fllng J, Inc.
If both of the items described above are answered in the affirmative, then
presumably the property will not be conveyed to Mr. Pistacchio, but rather to Flying J,
Inc. If either of the two items is answered in the negative, then it is possible that at some
later date the Transportation Commission will be asked to approve the conveyance of the
property to Mr. Pistacchio.
During the times relevant to this inquiry, Commissioner Lindsey has been in the
trucking business. He is and has been the 100% owner and president of Brite Transport
System, Inc. ("Brite"). Brite and another corporation, Ralph Pipkin Trucking, Inc.
("Pipkin") are 50Yo partners in a trucking business known as B & P Bulk ("B & P').
Commissioner Lindsey manages B & P. There is no other commingling or sharing of
resources or staff as between Brite and Pipkin, other than the B & P partnership and
management of B & P by the commissioner. Neither Brite nor Pipkin is listed on any


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 3
exchange, neither has a net income of $500,000 or more, and neither has earnings before
taxes of $750,000 or more.
Prior to 1980, Mr. Pistacchio, directly or through one of his companies, hauled
commodities as a subcontractor to either Brite or B & P and received fees paid to him in
exchange for hauling those commodities. Since 1980, so far as Commissioner Lindsey is
aware, there has been no relationship between Mr. Pistacchio and any of Mr. Pistacchio's
companies, on the one hand, and Commissioner Lindsey, Brite, or B & P on the other
hand.
Flying J, Inc. operates truck stops across the United States at which it sells fuel
and other products. Until about ten years ago, B & P operated trucks in Southern
California and purchased fuel and other products from Flying J truck stops. However,
about ten years ago B & P ceased operating trucks in Southem California and terminated
fuel purchases from Flying J, Inc.
Commissioner Lindsey has no specific knowledge as to how the property will be
developed, if at all. Flying J, Inc. may develop the property at issue as a truck stop.
However, Commissioner Lindsey has no knowledge as to the plans which Mr. Pistacchio
mayhave for the property.
Currently, fuel purchased for trucks of Brite and B & P is purchased pursuant to a
bulk tank conkact so that fuel is not purchased from truck stops. In any case, because
these trucks get fuel in Hanford, California, the trucks do not fuel in Mojave where the
caltrans property is located, nor are they expected to do so in the future.
AIIALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported thern." (Section S1001(b).) tn furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis as outlined below.3
Steps One and Two: Is Commissioner Lindsey considered a 66public official,,
making participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the California Transportation Commission, Kirk Lindsey is a
"member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency'' and,
3 This analysis is based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation 18700(b).


File No A-0fi3:;
therefore, a public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 8701 (a).)
A public official "makes a govefirmental decision" when the o{ficial, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702J)
Commissioner Lindsey will "make a govemmental decision" if he votes on a
decision to reconsider or approve a conveyance by Caltrans of excess higlrway property.
Additionally, if he engages in any of the actions described in enclosed regulations
18702.2 and 18702.3 with regard to such decisions, that will constitute "participating in
making" or "influencing" that decision.
Step Three: What are Commissioner Lindsey's economic interests - 
the possible
sources ofa conflict ofinterest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
goveflrmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent a of $2,000 or more (section 37103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1O));
a
a
A public ofEcial has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 ormore (section S7103(b); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomes, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 1 8703.3);
o An indirect investnent or interest means any investunent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
s Please note that the "[i]ncome of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a
10-percent interest or greater." (Section 82030.)


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 5
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
871 03(e); regulation 187 B.$;
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703.5).
In addition, "[a]n official has an economic interest in a business entity which is a
parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in which the official"
has an economic interest. (Regulation I 8703. 1 (c).) "A parent-subsidiary relationship
exists when one corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50
percent of the voting power of another corporation." (Regulation 18703.1(dxl).)
"Business entities, including corporations, partnerships,
joint ventures and any other organizations and enterprises
operated for profit, which do not have a parent-subsidiary
relationship are otherwise related if any one of the
following three tests is met:
(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership
interest in the other business entity.
(B) There is shared management and control between the
entities. In determining whether there is shared
management and control, consideration should be given to
the following factors:
(i) The same person or substantially the same person
owns and manages the two entities;
(ii) There are cofirmon or cofilmingled funds or assets;
(iii) The business entities share the use ofthe same
offices or ernployees, or otherwise share activities,
resources or personnel on a regular basis;
(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working
relationship between the entities; or
(C) A controlling owner (50o/o or greater interest as a
shareholder or as a general partner) in one entity also is a
controlling owner in the other entity."
(Regulation I 8703. 1 (dX2).)
Trucking Businesses
Provided Commissioner Lindsey has an investment of $2,000 or more in his
trucking business (Brite), he has an economic interest in this business entity. (He also
has an economic interest in this business as a source of income to him.)


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 6
Additionally, by virtue of Brite's 50% ownership interest in B & P, he has an
economic interest in B & P, a business entity "otherwise related" to Brite. (See
regulation 1 8703. 1 (d) (2)(C) above.)
Customers of Trucking Businesses
Because the income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income
of any business entity in which Commissioner Lindsey owns a l0-percent or greater
interest (section 82030), each customer of Brite from whom he has received $500 or
more is a source of income to him. (See regulation 18703.3(a).)
Under this rule, 50Yo of the income received by B & P will also be attributed to
commissioner Lindsey. Therefore, any customer of B & P from whom income
aggregating $500 or more is attributed to Commissioner Lindsey, will be a source of
income to the commissioner. For example, if a customer paid B & P $1,000, then 50o/o
(or $500) of the payment will be attributed to Brite. Since Commissioner Lindsey is a
100% owner of Brite, 100% (or $500) of the payment attributed to Brite will in tum be
attributed to the commissioner, and he will have an economic interest in that particular
customerofB&P.
You have not identified particular customers of Commissioner Lindsey's business
nor provided specific information on their real properties or businesses in order for us to
conduct a complete analysis as to whether a conflict of interest will arise from any of
thern. Therefore, we are able to provide only general assistance with regard to the
customers of his business.6
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests of the
commissioner's. For purposes of this letter, we assume he has no other economic
interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.
Please note that Commissioner Lindsey does not have an economic interest in
either Flying J, Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio the under provisions of the Act.
Step Four: Are Commissioner Lindsey's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
Trucking Businesses/Customers
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
6 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regulations section 183)9(cX3),
copy enclosed.)


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 7
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appteal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
(Regulation 1 8704. 1 (a).)
Since neither Brite, B & P, nor any of the customers of these businesses initiated
the proceeding in which decisions regarding the conveyance will be made, Commissioner
Lindsey's economic interests would be indirectly involved for purposes of the Act.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Trucking Businesses
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies to sources of income which are business entities
indirectly involved in a decision. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(1).)
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards applicable to business
entities indirectly involved in a decision. Subdivision (c)(l) ofregulation 18705.1
applies only where a business entity is listed on the Fortune 500. Subdivisions (cX2) -
(3) of regulation 18705.1 apply where a business entity is listed on a certain exchange
(e.9, the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ, or the American Stock Exchange);
in addition, subdivisions (c)(2) {cX3) apply where a business entity is not listed on one
of the specified exchanges but altemate eamings/income criteria for the entity are met.
Finally, subdivision (cX4) applies to business entities, such as small businesses, not
covered by subdivisions (c)(l )-(3).
Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision
on a small business such as Brite or B & P is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that :
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govenrmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 13705.1(cXa).)


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 8
If it is not reasonably foreseeable that any of these monetary amounts will be met, then
the commissioner will not have a conflict of interest in the decisions you identified.
Customers of Trucking Businesses
In general for the decisions you have described, where an indirectly involved
source of income (a customer) is a business entity, apply regulation 18705.1(c) set forth
above. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(1).) Where the source of income is an individual, apply
regulation 18705.3(b)(3), copy enclosed. Where the source of income is a nonprofit
entity, apply 18705.3(b)(2). Essentially, to determine whether he has a conflict of
interest arising from any one of his customers, Commissioner Lindsey must identif,i each
customer from whom he has received $500 and, with that information, identifli the
applicable materiality standard for each customer.
Without further information on what type of customers Brite and B& P has (i.e.,
whether they are individuals or businesses or nonprofit entities) and, if entities, what size
they are, we cannot identifu the applicable materiality standards for these customers in
order to complete our analysis.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon any of your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?
After identifying the applicable materiality standards, Commissioner Lindsey
must then assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect his
economic interests in an amount that rises to the level considered material for that
particular economic interest. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the monetary threshold
will be met as a result of the decision, then the commissioner will have a conflict of
interest arising from that economic interest and will be prohibited from participating in
the decision.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. Qn re Tltorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Trucking Businesses
Commissioner Lindsey must assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decisions in question will materially affect his business's expenses, assets, liabilities, or
gross revenues by the amounts specified in regulation 18705.1(c)(4). (See above.)
The question of whether financial consequences on a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time of a governmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Thus, we cannot
categorically state at this time whether the decisions you have identified will or will not


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 9
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Commissioner Lindsey's
businesses. Nevertheless, the Thorner opinion discusses facts similar to those you
present which are instructive in the present circumstances.
In the Thorner opinion, the Commission compared a situation where a contractor
who was a regular customer of the public official's hardware business was preparing to
bid on, or had bid on, a project that was the subject of a decision before the official, to a
scenario where the contractor was already awarded the contract, but had not yet
purchased or agreed to purchase any of the official's materials for the project. The
Commission stated:
"There is a significant difference between the two situations....[A]lthough
there is no certainty that McPhail's will receive business, there is a high
probability that it will since the contractor who has been awarded the
contract is a regular customer. Although there is no agreement, express or
implied [citation omitted] between McPhail's and the contractor, there is,
without question, a sufficient likelihood that McPhail's will receive
business to make the financial effect on Director McPhail 'reasonably
foreseeable.'
"...[In the other situation,] an extra degree of remoteness is added to the
foreseeability of the financial effect by reason of the fact that the
contractor has not yet been awarded the contract, but merely has entered a
bid or is preparing to do so. McPhail's will benefit only if the contractor's
bid is successful and the contractor follows its normal practice of
purchasing from McPhail's."
This "extra degree of remoteness," the Commission concluded, meant that the financial
efFect was not reasonably foreseeable. (Thiltgen Advice Letter, No. I-03-053.)
ln the commissioner's situation, his businesses will not be affected unless several
events occur: First, the property must be conveyed to a person who will develop it.
Second, the property must be developed in such away that it could create a financial
effect on his businesses. Third, his businesses must engage in business activities (e.g.,
purchasing fuel from a truck stop built on the property) which would result in a positive
or negative financial affect on the businesses. Finally, the financial effect must amount to
one of the monetary thresholds of regulation 18705.1(cX4).
The facts you provide exhibit the "extra degree of remoteness" which, in the
Thorner opinion, indicates that decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect upon an official's economic interest. (See Olson Advice Letter, No. 4-00-237.) In
particular, you have stated that Commissioner Lindsey has no specific knowledge as to
how the property will be developed, if at all, regardless of whether the property is
conveyed to either Flying J, Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio. However, even if the parcel is
developed as a truck stop, there would be no financial effect on Commissioner Lindsey's


File No. A-04-A25
Page No. 10
businesses since the trucks of these businesses do not fuel in Mojave where the Caltrans
property is located, nor are they expected to do so in the future.
Thus, unless he is aware that the conveyance of the property to either Flying J,
Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio will ultimately create a financial effect on either of his businesses
or on another economic interest of his which meets an applicable materiality standard, the
element of reasonable foreseeability necessary for the existence of a conflict of interest
will be lacking.
Nevertheless, the question of whether financial consequences on a business entity
are reasonably foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Because the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice Qn re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable that an
applicable materiality standard will be met for any of Commissioner Lindsey's economic
interests, including the customers of his businesses (see below), is necessarily a factual
question that is ultimately for Commissioner Lindsey to decide.
Customers of Trucking Businesses
Once Commissioner Lindsey has identified the applicable materiality standard for
each customer as described above, he must next assess whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met. Specifically, he must
assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision regarding the
proposed conveyance will result in a certain dollar effect (material effect) on each
customer with whom he has an economic interest.
Please be aware that we are unable to provide formal advice with regard to any
conflict of interest arising from Commissioner Lindsey's economic interest in customers
to his businesses based on the information you have provided. Consequently our
guidance with respect to these customers'is limited to informal assistance.T
steps seven and Eight: The 6'Public Generally" and *Legally Required" Exceptions
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "public generally''exception
or the "legally required participation" rule, exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, are
applicable to your situation.
' O.,ly formal written advice confers immunity provided under section 83 I l4(b) and regulation
1 83 29 (b) (7 ), enc lo sed.


File No. A-04-025
Page No. 11
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

that is ultimately for Commissioner Lindsey to decide.
Customers of Trucking Businesses
Once Commissioner Lindsey has identified the applicable materiality standard for

foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met. Specifically, he must
assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision regarding the
proposed conveyance will result in a certain dollar effect (material effect) on each
customer with whom he has an economic interest.
Please be aware that we are unable to provide formal advice with regard to any

to his businesses based on the information you have provided. Consequently our

sources ofa conflict ofinterest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
goveflrmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investrnent a of $2,000 or more (section 37103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation
18703.1O));
a
a
A public ofEcial has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 ormore (section S7103(b); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomes, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 1 8703.3);
o An indirect investnent or interest means any investunent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
s Please note that the "[i]ncome of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of
any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a
10-percent interest or greater." (Section 82030.)
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
871 03(e); regulation 187 B.$;
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 13703.5).
In addition, "[a]n official has an economic interest in a business entity which is a
parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in which the official"
has an economic interest. (Regulation I 8703. 1 (c).) "A parent-subsidiary relationship
exists when one corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50
percent of the voting power of another corporation." (Regulation 18703.1(dxl).)
"Business entities, including corporations, partnerships,
joint ventures and any other organizations and enterprises
operated for profit, which do not have a parent-subsidiary
relationship are otherwise related if any one of the
following three tests is met:
(A) One business entity has a controlling ownership
interest in the other business entity.
(B) There is shared management and control between the
entities. In determining whether there is shared
management and control, consideration should be given to
the following factors:
(i) The same person or substantially the same person
owns and manages the two entities;
(ii) There are cofirmon or cofilmingled funds or assets;
(iii) The business entities share the use ofthe same
offices or ernployees, or otherwise share activities,
resources or personnel on a regular basis;
(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working
relationship between the entities; or
(C) A controlling owner (50o/o or greater interest as a
shareholder or as a general partner) in one entity also is a
controlling owner in the other entity."
(Regulation I 8703. 1 (dX2).)
Trucking Businesses
Provided Commissioner Lindsey has an investment of $2,000 or more in his
trucking business (Brite), he has an economic interest in this business entity. (He also
has an economic interest in this business as a source of income to him.)
Additionally, by virtue of Brite's 50% ownership interest in B & P, he has an
economic interest in B & P, a business entity "otherwise related" to Brite. (See
regulation 1 8703. 1 (d) (2)(C) above.)
Customers of Trucking Businesses
Because the income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any income
of any business entity in which Commissioner Lindsey owns a l0-percent or greater
interest (section 82030), each customer of Brite from whom he has received $500 or
more is a source of income to him. (See regulation 18703.3(a).)
Under this rule, 50Yo of the income received by B & P will also be attributed to
commissioner Lindsey. Therefore, any customer of B & P from whom income
aggregating $500 or more is attributed to Commissioner Lindsey, will be a source of
income to the commissioner. For example, if a customer paid B & P $1,000, then 50o/o
(or $500) of the payment will be attributed to Brite. Since Commissioner Lindsey is a
100% owner of Brite, 100% (or $500) of the payment attributed to Brite will in tum be
attributed to the commissioner, and he will have an economic interest in that particular
customerofB&P.
You have not identified particular customers of Commissioner Lindsey's business
nor provided specific information on their real properties or businesses in order for us to
conduct a complete analysis as to whether a conflict of interest will arise from any of
thern. Therefore, we are able to provide only general assistance with regard to the
customers of his business.6
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests of the
commissioner's. For purposes of this letter, we assume he has no other economic
interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.
Please note that Commissioner Lindsey does not have an economic interest in
either Flying J, Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio the under provisions of the Act.
Step Four: Are Commissioner Lindsey's economic interests directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
Trucking Businesses/Customers
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of
income, and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person,
either directly or by an agent:
6 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regulations section 183)9(cX3),
copy enclosed.)
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appteal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
(Regulation 1 8704. 1 (a).)
Since neither Brite, B & P, nor any of the customers of these businesses initiated
the proceeding in which decisions regarding the conveyance will be made, Commissioner
Lindsey's economic interests would be indirectly involved for purposes of the Act.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Trucking Businesses
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies to sources of income which are business entities
indirectly involved in a decision. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(1).)
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards applicable to business
entities indirectly involved in a decision. Subdivision (c)(l) ofregulation 18705.1
applies only where a business entity is listed on the Fortune 500. Subdivisions (cX2) -
(3) of regulation 18705.1 apply where a business entity is listed on a certain exchange
(e.9, the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ, or the American Stock Exchange);
in addition, subdivisions (c)(2) {cX3) apply where a business entity is not listed on one
of the specified exchanges but altemate eamings/income criteria for the entity are met.
Finally, subdivision (cX4) applies to business entities, such as small businesses, not
covered by subdivisions (c)(l )-(3).
Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision
on a small business such as Brite or B & P is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that :
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govenrmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 13705.1(cXa).)
If it is not reasonably foreseeable that any of these monetary amounts will be met, then
the commissioner will not have a conflict of interest in the decisions you identified.
Customers of Trucking Businesses
In general for the decisions you have described, where an indirectly involved
source of income (a customer) is a business entity, apply regulation 18705.1(c) set forth
above. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(1).) Where the source of income is an individual, apply
regulation 18705.3(b)(3), copy enclosed. Where the source of income is a nonprofit
entity, apply 18705.3(b)(2). Essentially, to determine whether he has a conflict of
interest arising from any one of his customers, Commissioner Lindsey must identif,i each
customer from whom he has received $500 and, with that information, identifli the
applicable materiality standard for each customer.
Without further information on what type of customers Brite and B& P has (i.e.,
whether they are individuals or businesses or nonprofit entities) and, if entities, what size
they are, we cannot identifu the applicable materiality standards for these customers in
order to complete our analysis.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon any of your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?
After identifying the applicable materiality standards, Commissioner Lindsey
must then assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect his
economic interests in an amount that rises to the level considered material for that
particular economic interest. If it is reasonably foreseeable that the monetary threshold
will be met as a result of the decision, then the commissioner will have a conflict of
interest arising from that economic interest and will be prohibited from participating in
the decision.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. Qn re Tltorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Trucking Businesses
Commissioner Lindsey must assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decisions in question will materially affect his business's expenses, assets, liabilities, or
gross revenues by the amounts specified in regulation 18705.1(c)(4). (See above.)
The question of whether financial consequences on a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable at the time of a governmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Thus, we cannot
categorically state at this time whether the decisions you have identified will or will not
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Commissioner Lindsey's
businesses. Nevertheless, the Thorner opinion discusses facts similar to those you
present which are instructive in the present circumstances.
In the Thorner opinion, the Commission compared a situation where a contractor
who was a regular customer of the public official's hardware business was preparing to
bid on, or had bid on, a project that was the subject of a decision before the official, to a
scenario where the contractor was already awarded the contract, but had not yet
purchased or agreed to purchase any of the official's materials for the project. The
Commission stated:
"There is a significant difference between the two situations....[A]lthough
there is no certainty that McPhail's will receive business, there is a high
probability that it will since the contractor who has been awarded the
contract is a regular customer. Although there is no agreement, express or
implied [citation omitted] between McPhail's and the contractor, there is,
without question, a sufficient likelihood that McPhail's will receive
business to make the financial effect on Director McPhail 'reasonably
foreseeable.'
"...[In the other situation,] an extra degree of remoteness is added to the
foreseeability of the financial effect by reason of the fact that the
contractor has not yet been awarded the contract, but merely has entered a
bid or is preparing to do so. McPhail's will benefit only if the contractor's
bid is successful and the contractor follows its normal practice of
purchasing from McPhail's."
This "extra degree of remoteness," the Commission concluded, meant that the financial
efFect was not reasonably foreseeable. (Thiltgen Advice Letter, No. I-03-053.)
ln the commissioner's situation, his businesses will not be affected unless several
events occur: First, the property must be conveyed to a person who will develop it.
Second, the property must be developed in such away that it could create a financial
effect on his businesses. Third, his businesses must engage in business activities (e.g.,
purchasing fuel from a truck stop built on the property) which would result in a positive
or negative financial affect on the businesses. Finally, the financial effect must amount to
one of the monetary thresholds of regulation 18705.1(cX4).
The facts you provide exhibit the "extra degree of remoteness" which, in the
Thorner opinion, indicates that decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect upon an official's economic interest. (See Olson Advice Letter, No. 4-00-237.) In
particular, you have stated that Commissioner Lindsey has no specific knowledge as to
how the property will be developed, if at all, regardless of whether the property is
conveyed to either Flying J, Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio. However, even if the parcel is
developed as a truck stop, there would be no financial effect on Commissioner Lindsey's


File No. A-04-A25
businesses since the trucks of these businesses do not fuel in Mojave where the Caltrans
property is located, nor are they expected to do so in the future.
Thus, unless he is aware that the conveyance of the property to either Flying J,
Inc. or Mr. Pistacchio will ultimately create a financial effect on either of his businesses
or on another economic interest of his which meets an applicable materiality standard, the
element of reasonable foreseeability necessary for the existence of a conflict of interest
will be lacking.
Nevertheless, the question of whether financial consequences on a business entity
are reasonably foreseeable at the time a govemmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Because the
Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice Qn re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable that an
applicable materiality standard will be met for any of Commissioner Lindsey's economic
interests, including the customers of his businesses (see below), is necessarily a factual

QUESTION: that is ultimately for Commissioner Lindsey to decide.
Customers of Trucking Businesses
Once Commissioner Lindsey has identified the applicable materiality standard for
each customer as described above, he must next assess whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met. Specifically, he must
assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a particular decision regarding the
proposed conveyance will result in a certain dollar effect (material effect) on each
customer with whom he has an economic interest.
Please be aware that we are unable to provide formal advice with regard to any
conflict of interest arising from Commissioner Lindsey's economic interest in customers
to his businesses based on the information you have provided. Consequently our
guidance with respect to these customers'is limited to informal assistance.T
steps seven and Eight: The 6'Public Generally" and *Legally Required" Exceptions
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "public generally''exception
or the "legally required participation" rule, exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, are
applicable to your situation.
' O.,ly formal written advice confers immunity provided under section 83 I l4(b) and regulation
1 83 29 (b) (7 ), enc lo sed.

CONCLUSION: 1 - 2. Commissioner Lindsey may participate in these decisions provided it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will materially affect Commissioner Lindsey's
economic interests. (See discussion.)

Spanog Deputy Attorney General State of California Department of Justice Post Office 8ox944255 Sacramento, CA 94244-2550 Re: Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A-04-025 Dear Mr. Spanos: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Transportation Commission and Kirk Lindsay,r amernber of the California Transportation Commission, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").z Please note that the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation I 83 29(b)(8XA).) QUESTTONS May Commissioner Lindsey participate in the following decisions 1. Whether the Transportation Commission should reconsider the proposed conveyance of excess propert/ 2. If yes, whether the Transportation Commission should approve conveyance to Flying J, Inc.? CONCLUSIONS 1 - 2. Commissioner Lindsey may participate in these decisions provided it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will materially affect Commissioner Lindsey's economic interests. (See discussion.) t You a.e authorized by Commissioner Lindsey to request advice on this matter. ' Gorerrme.rt Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commiision regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ary$.41 File No. A-04-025 Page No. 2 FACTS The following facts are based on your correspondence and phone conversations with Commission staff counsel. You have requested advice regarding a property conveyance by the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"). Although the California Transportation Commission and Caltrans are separate state agencies, state law requires that any conveyance by Caltrans of excess property be approved by the Transportation Commission. In February 2003, Caltrans asked the Transportation Commission to approve an excess property conveyance to Flying J, Inc. The proposed conveyance was an element of a contract between Caltrans and Flying J, [nc. The Transportation Commission did not approve the conveyance, and instead voted (by a vote of 6 to 1) to have the property put out for competitive bidding. In May 2003, Caltrans conducted an auction of the property,located in Mojave near an interchange. The bidders were informed that the auction sale was the subject of litigation involving Calkans and Flying J, [nc. and was also subject to approval by the Transportation Commission. The high bidder was a mernber of the public, Thomas Pistacchio. Due to the pending litigation, Caltrans has not asked for approval of any conveyance of the property to Mr. Pistacchio. In addition, a request has been made that the Transportation Commission reconsider its February 2003 decision. The agenda for the Transportation Commission's February 26,2004, meeting includes consideration of two related items. The first item is whether the Transportation Commission should reconsider the proposed conveyance to Mr. Pistacchio. The second itern, which assumes that the first itern is answered in the affirmative, is whether the Transportation Commission should approve the proposed conveyance to Fllng J, Inc. If both of the items described above are answered in the affirmative, then presumably the property will not be conveyed
